source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
topic: App Intelligence Header and Archetype Relevance
summary: This chunk contains the core identity and archetype relevance matrix for Purple Dead-Nettle, outlining its roles as a pollinator resource and soil indicator. It categorizes how different land stewards can utilize or manage the plant based on their specific goals.
chunk_text:
--- LAYER 0 — APP INTELLIGENCE HEADER
Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
=== BLOCK 1: PLANT IDENTITY ===
Common Name: Purple dead-nettle
Scientific Name: Lamium purpureum L.
Family: Lamiaceae (mint family)
Profile Type: Living Plant Wisdom Profile
Version: 2.0
Source Document ID: purple_dead-nettle_wisdom_profile
Knowledge Tier: Tier 1 – Core Library (High Retrieval Priority)
=== BLOCK 2: ARCHETYPE RELEVANCE MATRIX ===
Primary Archetypes (HIGH relevance):
Market Gardener: Early-season pollinator support, volunteer cover cropping, and fresh spring green niche products.
Homesteader: Free spring biomass for compost, emergency famine food history, and traditional medicinal poultices.
Land Steward: Management of invasive pressure in sensitive ecosystems (Garry Oak) and monitoring disturbance indicators.
Secondary Archetypes (MODERATE relevance):
Homegrower: Identification of common "weeds" and understanding their role in soil protection and bee forage.
Consultant: Using the plant as a diagnostic tool for soil disturbance and advising on "leave-and-cut" pollinator strategies.
Tertiary Archetypes (LOW relevance):
Rancher: Low-palatability sheep forage; non-toxic but minimal nutritional value for livestock compared to primary pasture.
Transitioning Farmer: Understanding winter annual cycles and potential host relationships (Soybean Cyst Nematode).

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Covers the functional ecological roles of Purple Dead-Nettle, including its status as a high-value soil indicator and pollinator resource. It details the plant's soil preferences and its contribution to building soil health through nitrogen-rich biomass.
chunk_text:
=== BLOCK 3: ECOLOGICAL ROLE CLASSIFICATION ===
Functional Guild Roles:
Early-season pollinator forage (Pollen resource)
Opportunistic volunteer soil cover
Biomass accumulator (Rapid spring turnover)
Soil disturbance indicatorSuccession Stage: Early — An annual disturbance-follower that colonizes bare mineral soil.Soil Indicator Value: HIGH — Signals recent soil movement/cultivation, high light, and reasonable nutrient availability.Associated Species / Guild Partners:
Honey bees and solitary bees (Forage relationship)
Ants (Seed dispersal/Myrmecochory)
Henbit (Lamium amplexicaule) (Common look-alike/habitat partner)
=== BLOCK 4: SOIL LANGUAGE ===
Soil Preferences: Productive, well-lit, reasonably fertile, and disturbed ground; prefers mesic moisture.
pH Range: Slight tendency toward base-influenced soils.
Drainage: Prefers moist but not waterlogged (mesic) conditions.
Texture Affinity: Prefers "turned beds" and loose mineral soil; avoids hardpan or exposed ridgelines.
Soil Health Signal: Presence indicates "opened" soil ready for cover; absence from dry/degraded sites suggests low moisture-holding capacity.
Soil Building Contribution:
Returns nitrogen-rich biomass (approx. 10% crude protein) to the soil surface upon early summer senescence.
Provides surface protection against erosion during the spring transition.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
topic: Seasonal Windows, Medicinal Flags, and Enterprise Strategy
summary: Outlines the seasonal management calendar for Purple Dead-Nettle and its medicinal/nutritional profile. It also introduces enterprise strategies, highlighting revenue potential in specialty greens and the plant's role as a biological bridge for pollinators and soil.
chunk_text:
=== BLOCK 5: SEASONAL ACTION WINDOWS ===
Spring:
Peak flowering (Feb–March); monitor pollinator activity.
Harvest young tops for food (pre-flowering).
Cut/mow before seed set (April) to manage the seedbank.Summer:
Plant senesces and disappears; allow biomass to return to soil.
Avoid management as the plant is already dormant/dead.Fall:
Primary germination window (Oct–Nov); light cultivation triggers the next cohort.
Identify small rosettes for early-stage control if needed.Winter:
Slow vegetative growth; last window for easy physical removal (Jan–Feb) before rapid spring acceleration.
=== BLOCK 6: MEDICINAL AND NUTRITIONAL FLAGS ===
Nutritional Tier: MODERATE
Used historically as a famine food (stewed leaves) and modern culinary "New Nordic" ingredient.
High protein relative to biomass; vitamin/mineral claims are widespread but currently uncited in primary PNW literature.Key Medicinal Actions:
Analgesic (Poultice for pain relief)
Anti-inflammatory (Traditional decoction for sore throat/tonsillar inflammation)
Preclinical TNF-α inhibition and radical-scavenging activity.Safety Flags:
Heavy Metal Bioaccumulation: Risk of arsenic, lead, and mercury in plants from roadsides or industrial sites.
Taxonomic Blur: Ensure ID is not confused with other Lamium species if seeking specific medicinal outcomes.
Pregnancy/Lactation: Safety unknown; no clinical human trials exist.
=== BLOCK 7: ENTERPRISE INTELLIGENCE ===
Revenue Potential: LOW
Product Streams:
Fresh specialty spring greens (Boutique market/restaurant supply)
Dried culinary tisane/herbal tea blends
Educational foraging workshops/contentInput Cost: Near-zero — Seed and establishment are handled by the plant's opportunistic nature.Labor Intensity: Moderate — High labor if hand-weeding active beds;
low if managed via timed mowing in margins.
=== BLOCK 8: BRIDGE STRATEGY FLAGS ===
Extractive Pressure Scenarios This Plant Addresses:
Nutritional Scarcity: Bridge for overwintering pollinators before commercial/native flowers bloom.
Bare Soil Liability: Bridge for soil protection between autumn harvest and spring planting.Transition Framing:

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: A descriptive introduction to encountering Purple Dead-Nettle in the Pacific Northwest landscape. It provides key visual identification cues, such as square stems and hooded flowers, and explains the significance of the 'dead-nettle' name.
chunk_text:
Section 1 — Opening Encounter

Walk a field margin in late February or early March in the Pacific Northwest and you'll find it before you're looking for it.
A low flush of purple at the edge of the turned bed, a reddish smear along the roadside ditch, a soft mat of colour tucked against the base of a fence post where nobody thought to mow.
The plant is small and it doesn't announce itself. It simply appears, as if it materialized overnight from the cold mud, which — given how it germinates — is basically what happened.
Purple dead-nettle doesn't look like much from a distance. Get down on your knees and the picture changes.
The upper leaves are crowded and overlapping, flushed deep reddish-purple in full light, almost velvet to the eye.
Lower leaves are greener, heart-shaped, stalked, with scalloped edges that catch the morning dew.
The stems are square — this is the mint family, and the four-angled stem is its calling card — and they recline outward from the base rather than shooting straight up, giving the plant a low, spreading posture that suggests it's in no rush to be impressive.
It is, perhaps, 15 to 30 centimetres tall on a good day. Often less.
The flowers are easy to miss individually but impossible to overlook in aggregate.
They cluster in whorls near the stem tips, tucked between those purple-flushed upper leaves, small pink-purple tubes barely a centimetre long.
Each one is bilabiate — two-lipped — the upper lip forming a small hood, the lower lip spotted or streaked.
They open early and they don't wait for warm weather.
In southwestern BC and the Puget lowlands, flowering can begin in late February and continue, with enough moisture, well into spring.
By May, if the season turns dry, it's mostly done.
By summer it's gone, seed set and decomposing, as if it was never there.
It smells faintly of green — not mint-sharp, not unpleasant, just raw and vaguely herbal in the way that many plants in its family smell when you crush the leaves.
The leaves themselves have a soft, slightly rough texture, finely hairy, not unpleasant to touch.
Nothing about this plant bites back. That's the whole point of the name: dead-nettle.
It looks like a stinging nettle — the leaf shape, the hairy stems, the low spreading habit — but it carries no sting.
Not dead in the sense of absence, but dead in the sense of disarmed.
You'll find it wherever the soil has been moved and then left. Garden beds. Field margins. Roadsides.
The disturbed edges of construction sites. The strip of bare ground between a fence and a footpath.
It follows human disturbance like a slow-moving wake. In southwestern BC it's most frequent in the Fraser Delta lowlands and surrounding areas — the settled, farmed, churned-up ground where introduced plants have been arriving for over a century.
A few records push east of the Coast-Cascade divide, but it's primarily a coastal and lowland plant here, comfortable in the mild, wet winters that allow it to germinate almost year-round when conditions are right.
It matters because it's first. Before the willows fully leaf, before the dandelions get going, before the orchard flowers open — this plant is blooming.
And bloom in early spring, in a landscape that has been stripped and turned and repeatedly disturbed, is not a small thing.
The bees need to eat too.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Explores the taxonomic lineage of Lamium purpureum, its global distribution as a companion to human agriculture, and the importance of distinguishing it from look-alikes like henbit. It also touches on historical medical references and the genomic stability of the species.
chunk_text:
Section 3 — Identity and Lineage

The name is a small puzzle worth unpacking. Lamium comes from the Greek laimos, meaning throat — a reference to the tubular, throat-like shape of the flower.
Purpureum simply means purple. So the formal name gives you a purple-throated flower, which is accurate enough, though it undersells the plant's more interesting qualities.
The common name, dead-nettle, has been doing its explanatory work for a long time.
It shows up in old English plant literature as a way of distinguishing this plant — harmless, soft-stemmed, without any chemical defence — from the stinging nettles it resembles in leaf shape and general growth form.
Dead:
ere is not a comment on the plant's vitality.
It is, rather, a statement about what the plant lacks: the hollow stinging trichomes, filled with formic acid and other compounds, that make Urtica species so memorably unpleasant to brush against.
The dead-nettle looks the part but is bluffing. It has the leaf architecture of a nettle without the consequences.
Taxonomically, Lamium purpureum sits squarely in the Lamiaceae — the mint family — one of the most ecologically and ethnobotanically significant plant families on earth.
Mints, sages, thymes, lavenders, basils, oreganos: this family has been feeding, healing, and perfuming human life for millennia.
Lamium is one of the older, wilder relatives — no culinary fame, no essential-oil industry built around it, just a genus of low-growing herbs that colonize disturbed ground and bloom when little else does.
The species was formally described by Linnaeus in 1753, making it one of the original entries in the canonical botanical record.
Its native range is broad: Macaronesia and the Mediterranean coast, across Europe, east through West Asia and into West Siberia.
It has, in other words, been a companion plant to human agriculture and settlement across a very wide swath of the temperate world for a very long time.
When European settlers came to North America, the plant — or its seeds — came with them.
It is now recorded as introduced across much of temperate North America, including British Columbia, Ontario, and Quebec.
In BC, two varieties are recognized. Var. purpureum has round-toothed leaves. Var.
incisum has deeply, sharply incised leaves and is described in eFlora BC as
considered:
o be of hybrid origin between L. moschatum and L. purpureum — a parentage that has not been definitively settled by genetic work.
Both are present in the province; both behave similarly in the landscape.
Practically speaking, you can distinguish them by leaf-tooth character if it matters for your purposes, but both will do the same ecological work in your garden margin.
The chromosome count is 2n = 18, confirmed across multiple cytogenetic studies from Turkey and Japan — a stable, diploid plant with no unusual genomic complexity to speak of.
The look-alike situation deserves real attention. Lamium amplexicaule — henbit deadnettle — is the most likely source of confusion and the most important one to resolve.
It often grows in the same disturbed habitats at the same time of year.
The two are similar enough that they're frequently treated together in weed management guides.
The critical distinction is in the leaves: purple dead-nettle has stalked lower leaves and distinctly purple-flushed upper leaves in whorls;
henbit has clasping, stalkless upper leaves that wrap around the stem.
Get that character into your eye and you'll separate them reliably.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Describes the rapid life cycle of Purple Dead-Nettle as a winter annual. It explains how the plant utilizes shallow seed burial and soil disturbance to germinate, its ability to root at stem nodes in moist conditions, and its strategy of returning biomass to the soil before summer.
chunk_text:
Section 4 — Botanical Life

Purple dead-nettle lives fast and dies early. It is an annual — one growing season, seed to seed, no overwintering root — and in the Pacific Northwest context it typically behaves as a winter annual, which means it germinates in autumn or early spring, grows through the cool months, flowers, sets seed, and senesces before summer heat arrives.
In some conditions and climates it will also germinate in spring and complete its cycle quickly.
Either way, it's a short-season plant whose timing is tightly calibrated to the gap between cold dormancy and summer competition.
The seed is the beginning of everything. Lamium purpureum seeds have a documented dormancy cycle — weed scientists have studied their emergence patterns in relation to seasonal temperature and moisture shifts, and the seeds are capable of persisting in the seedbank, though how long they remain viability under Pacific Northwest conditions is not well established in the primary literature for this region.
What is clear is that germination is triggered by relatively shallow burial — around half a centimetre to two centimetres — and that soil disturbance dramatically increases germination rate by bringing seeds into that optimal zone.
This is why the plant tracks cultivation so closely. Every time you turn soil, you're essentially issuing an invitation.
The seedling emerges and establishes a short taproot — the anchoring system is modest, which matters for management — and then begins to branch from the base, sending out multiple stems that recline outward in a low, spreading rosette.
The stems are square in cross-section (again: mint family), inconspicuously hairy, often purple-tinged, typically reaching 10 to 40 centimetres as the plant matures.
The leaves are opposite, heart-shaped at the base, with toothed margins and leaf stalks on the lower leaves;
the upper leaves become crowded and sessile-looking in the flowering whorls, and in good light they flush deeply reddish-purple — a pigmentation shift that is one of the plant's most distinctive visual cues, though it can be subtle in shade.
The flowers open in whorls near the stem tips, pink-purple, tubular, about 10 to 15 millimetres long, with the characteristic bilabiate structure of the mint family — an upper hood, a lower lip.
They are capable of self-pollination, which guarantees seed set even in the absence of pollinators, but they also attract and reward bee visitors (more on this in the next section).
After pollination, each flower produces four small nutlets — the characteristic fruit type of the Lamiaceae — which contain the seeds for the next generation.
One aspect of the plant's biology that warrants attention for anyone managing it in gardens or beds: the stems are capable of rooting at nodes in cool, moist conditions.
This means a plant you pull and leave lying on damp soil can re-establish.
It also means that stems in contact with the ground can send out roots and extend the plant's footprint.
For a winter annual, this is a surprisingly persistent strategy.
It's not the same as true vegetative reproduction — this plant doesn't spread via runners or rhizomes the way some perennial weeds do — but it complicates the "just pull it" approach if the pulled material is left in place.
After seed set, the plant declines quickly. By early summer in most Pacific Northwest locations, it's gone — collapsed, desiccating, returning its biomass to the soil surface.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Examines Purple Dead-Nettle as a disturbance-responsive system. It provides quantitative data on its significant early-spring pollinator contribution, discusses its potential role as a host for soybean cyst nematodes, and addresses its invasive status in sensitive Garry Oak ecosystems.
chunk_text:
Section 5 — Ecological Intelligence

To understand what purple dead-nettle is doing in the landscape, you have to stop looking at it as a problem to be solved and start looking at it as a system responding to information.
It appears where soil has been disturbed. That is not coincidence — it is precision.
Lamium purpureum is calibrated, through millennia of co-evolution with agricultural and disturbed-ground habitats, to read exactly the signal that cultivation sends: exposed mineral soil, increased light at the surface, reduced competition from perennial vegetation.
The germination surge that follows soil disturbance is not the plant being opportunistic in a pejorative sense.
It is the plant correctly interpreting a set of environmental cues and responding appropriately. The soil has been opened.
Something needs to cover it. Purple dead-nettle volunteers.
European ecological indicator datasets — derived from expert-aggregated plot data across the continent — give a useful, if not directly transferable, picture of where this plant thrives.
High light values. Above-average nutrient associations. Mesic moisture conditions. A slight tendency toward base-influenced soils.
These numbers shouldn't be treated as a soil test — they're European summaries, and Pacific Northwest conditions differ — but they point toward a plant that is particularly at home in productive, well-lit, reasonably fertile, disturbed ground.
In other words: your garden bed in March.
The soil role of this plant is understudied in the scientific literature, at least for Pacific Northwest conditions.
What we can say is that the plant's biomass — a low mat of relatively nitrogen-containing tissue, given its measured crude protein content of around 10% dry matter — decomposes quickly once the plant senesces.
A stand of purple dead-nettle completing its cycle and collapsing into the soil surface in May or June is contributing organic matter, releasing nutrients, and — potentially — providing some surface protection against erosion and moisture loss during the transition from spring growth to summer bare soil.
Whether it significantly affects soil biology, fungal networks, or rhizosphere chemistry in meaningful ways is genuinely unknown for this region.
What is documented, and documented carefully, is the pollinator role.
A quantitative study from non-cropped habitats in southeastern Poland — the best species-level data I've found — measured Lamium purpureum as producing a mean of 8,500 floral units per square metre at peak density, with a low pollen mass per floral unit (0.02 milligrams).
The math on this is important: each individual flower contributes a small amount of pollen, but a dense stand contributes meaningfully in aggregate.
Honey bees have been documented collecting the plant's distinctive red pollen.
Extension observations from Michigan note bumblebee visitation, though with some uncertainty about how important this plant is relative to native early-season forbs for broader bee communities.
A Turkish bee-pollen study found Lamium purpureum pollen among the highest in protein content across measured taxa — relevant to bee colony nutrition, though this data comes from a different climate and the implications for Pacific Northwest populations aren't confirmed.
The timing is what makes this pollinator contribution significant regardless of the absolute numbers.
Purple dead-nettle flowers in late February and March in southwestern BC — sometimes earlier in mild years.
That is a period of genuine nutritional scarcity for overwintering bee colonies and for native solitary bees emerging from winter dormancy.
There is also the seed dispersal story, which adds another layer to the plant's community relationships.
There is credible botanical literature suggesting that Lamium seeds, like those of several other spring ephemerals, carry elaiosomes — small, lipid-rich structures that attract ants.
One ecological impact that is clearly documented, though in a different agricultural context: purple dead-nettle can support the reproduction of soybean cyst nematode (SCN) in US soybean systems.
There is also the Garry Oak question. In BC's endangered Garry Oak ecosystems — the dry, rocky, species-rich plant communities of southeastern Vancouver Island and the Gulf Islands — purple dead-nettle is treated as an invasive alien with the potential to displace native species in disturbed patches.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Analyzes the unique resilience strategy of Purple Dead-Nettle, which exploits the cool shoulder seasons while avoiding summer competition. It highlights how climate change may expand its operational window and emphasizes its reliable role as a pollinator anchor in unpredictable springs.
chunk_text:
Section 6 — Climate and Resilience

Purple dead-nettle is not a tough plant in the way we usually mean that word. It doesn't endure heat.
It doesn't push through drought. It doesn't colonize exposed ridgelines or hardpan subsoil.
Its resilience is of a different and, in some ways, more instructive kind: it is exquisitely calibrated to a specific window of opportunity, and it exploits that window with extraordinary efficiency.
The window is the cool, moist, shoulder season — autumn through early spring — when temperatures are too low for most competitors but not low enough to prevent germination and slow growth.
Lamium purpureum is a cold-tolerant annual. It can germinate at soil temperatures that would leave most garden crops dormant, and it can grow, flower, and set seed in weather that feels, to most farmers, like it belongs to the off-season.
In BC's coastal lowlands, where winters are mild and rarely deeply frozen, this gives the plant a long operational window — sometimes nearly year-round in sheltered spots with regular soil moisture.
What it cannot tolerate is summer. Heat and drought terminate it quickly.
A stand of purple dead-nettle that looks vigorous in April will be collapsed and desiccating by June in most Pacific Northwest locations.
This is not a failure of resilience — it is the strategy.
The plant has no interest in competing with the summer vegetation community.
It completes its entire life cycle in the gap before that community establishes, then surrenders the ground.
This is, in ecological terms, a form of temporal niche partitioning: avoiding competition by not being present when competition is strongest.
Cold hardiness appears to be meaningful but not extreme. The plant can survive light frosts — indeed, it routinely grows through Pacific Northwest winters where temperatures drop below freezing regularly — but hard freezes will knock it back.
Snow cover can protect low-growing plants by insulating them from the worst cold.
This is consistent with its native range, which spans the Mediterranean through to West Siberia, a range that includes serious continental winters but centres on mild temperate climates.
Disturbance is, paradoxically, both a stress and a requirement. Without disturbance — without the soil opening that exposes seeds and reduces competition — purple dead-nettle doesn't get established in the first place.
But once established, additional disturbance during the growing period can set it back or eliminate it entirely.
The plant's response to disturbance is therefore asymmetric: it needs disturbance to germinate, but it is vulnerable to disturbance once growing.
This asymmetry has direct management implications that we'll cover in the farm applications section.
What does this profile mean for future land stewardship in a changing climate? Two things worth considering.
First, as Pacific Northwest winters become milder and wetter — a well-documented trend in climate projections for this region — the window for winter annual weeds like purple dead-nettle is likely to expand rather than contract.
Warmer winters mean longer growing seasons for cold-tolerant species, more germination events per year, and reduced competition from cold-intolerant plants that previously limited their spread.
If you're managing for native plant communities in sensitive ecosystems, this is a pressure that will increase, not stabilize.
Second, and less obviously: the pollinator bridging role that this plant plays — early-season forage before most cultivated or native flowers are available — becomes more, not less, valuable in a landscape where climate disruption is decoupling the timing between bee emergence and flower availability.
A plant that blooms in February, regardless of what else is happening, is an anchor point.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Provides a month-by-month working calendar for Purple Dead-Nettle in the Pacific Northwest. It identifies critical windows for germination, easy physical removal, peak pollinator support, and the harvest of young tops for food while cautioning against seedbank accumulation.
chunk_text:
Section 7 — Seasonal Timing and Working Calendar

The most important thing to understand about purple dead-nettle's seasonal calendar is that it operates almost entirely in the months when most growers have their attention elsewhere.
This is part of why it arrives unnoticed, does its work, and sets seed before anyone has decided what to do about it.
Here is the working calendar for southwestern BC and the broader Pacific Northwest lowlands, adjusted for the plant's documented behavior in this region:
October – November: Germination begins in earnest as soils cool and autumn rains restore moisture to dried summer soils.
This is the primary germination window for the winter annual cohort.
Seeds are shallow — they need to be within about two centimetres of the surface — so any autumn cultivation or soil movement in October dramatically increases the density of the spring population.
If you are preparing beds in fall, you are sowing purple dead-nettle, whether you intend to or not.
November – January: Slow vegetative growth through the coldest months. Plants are small, low, often overlooked.
This is the period when they are most vulnerable and easiest to manage, but also when most growers are least likely to be paying attention to weed pressure.
A light cultivation or hand-pull of small seedlings at this stage costs almost nothing and prevents the spring flush entirely.
Late January – February: Growth accelerates as day length increases and temperatures begin to climb.
Plants that overwintered as seedlings begin to branch and bulk up quickly.
In mild coastal winters, this acceleration can begin surprisingly early — late January is not unusual in the Fraser Delta or on southern Vancouver Island.
This is the last practical window for easy physical removal before flowering begins.
Late February – March: Peak flowering in southwestern BC.
This is when the purple flush becomes conspicuous — large patches visible in field margins, garden beds, disturbed road shoulders.
This is also when the plant's pollinator value peaks. Honey bees are working these flowers on warm days, collecting the distinctive red pollen.
If your management goal is pollinator support, this is the period to leave undisturbed patches in place, away from sensitive ecosystem contexts.
March – April: Seed set begins. The plant is still flowering at the tips while setting seed at the base — a rolling production line of seeds throughout this period.
Once seed set begins in earnest, the management calculus shifts: removing or cutting the plant after this point contributes seeds to the seedbank even if you remove the parent plant.
Timing matters. The BC Garry Oak ecosystem guidance recommends cutting stems prior to flowering to prevent seedbank inputs — that window is January to late February for most coastal BC locations.
April – May: Senescence. The plant begins to collapse as temperatures rise and soils dry.
By mid-May in most lowland PNW locations, the above-ground biomass is in decline.
If you are using the plant as a compost or chop-and-drop input, harvesting the green biomass before or just at the onset of flowering — when protein content is highest and before seed has set — is the ideal timing.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Traces the cultural history of Purple Dead-Nettle from Anglo-Saxon medical charms to Swedish famine foods. It explores the plant's role in the contemporary 'New Nordic' culinary movement and documents living medicinal practices in Turkey, while highlighting the lack of documented Indigenous TEK in the Pacific Northwest.
chunk_text:
Section 8 — History, Folklore, and Cultural Memory

A plant that has been following humans and their disturbed ground around Europe and the Near East for millennia accumulates a layered history — part folk medicine, part famine food, part everyday background flora — that rarely makes it into the formal record cleanly.
Purple dead-nettle is that kind of plant. It was never an aristocrat of the herb garden.
It was the weed at the edge of the field that people quietly knew things about, whose knowledge lived in households and hands rather than in botanical treatises.
The oldest specific thread I can follow with any confidence is the Anglo-Saxon one, and even there the ground is uncertain.
The Lacnunga — a collection of Old English medical charms and remedies compiled around the tenth or eleventh century — includes a remedy known as Wið færstice, meaning "against a sudden stabbing pain."
The recipe calls for boiling feverfew, red nettle (seo reade netele), and plantain in butter to make a salve.
The scholar Alaric Hall, drawing on the work of M.L. Cameron, identifies this "red nettle" as Lamium purpureum.
The famine food thread is clearer and, in its own way, more revealing.
An eighteenth-century Swedish historical record, synthesized in a 2024 food history study, lists Lamium purpureum leaves prepared as a stew among the plants that rural populations turned to when conventional crops failed.
This is a pattern repeated across European ethnobotany: the weeds of the field margin become emergency nutrition when the field itself fails.
In the contemporary wild food movement — a different kind of cultural memory, but cultural memory nonetheless — purple dead-nettle appears as an edible green in European foraging guides and, notably, in records of wild food plant use in twenty-first century European contexts, including supply to restaurants as part of the "new Nordic" and broader foraged-ingredient movement.
The name "purple archangel" — one of the plant's less common English names — connects it to the broader Lamium tradition of "archangel" names, which were applied to various dead-nettles in European folk naming and are thought to reflect old beliefs about these plants' protective or healing properties.
In Northwest Turkey's Marmara region, fieldwork conducted in the early 2000s documented living use of Lamium purpureum specifically: aerial parts used as a poultice for pain relief, and as a decoction for sore throat and tonsillar inflammation.
This is ethnobotanical documentation at the gold standard — fieldwork, named species, named region, named preparation.
Section 9 — TEK and Stewardship

Purple dead-nettle is not a plant with a documented Indigenous knowledge tradition in what is now British Columbia or the broader Pacific Northwest.
It is an introduced species — it arrived with European settlement, spread through disturbed and cultivated ground, and established itself in a landscape where it had no prior ecological or cultural presence.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Details the sophisticated biochemical toolkit of Purple Dead-Nettle, focusing on pharmacologically significant compound classes like iridoid glycosides and phenolic acids. It addresses the conflicting data on volatile oils and the high protein content of the plant's pollen, while identifying major research gaps for Pacific Northwest populations.
chunk_text:
Section 11 — Nutritional and Chemical Architecture

The chemistry of purple dead-nettle is genuinely interesting, which is easy to miss because the plant looks so unremarkable.
But when you look at what's actually been identified in its tissues, a picture emerges of a plant carrying a reasonably sophisticated biochemical toolkit — one that makes coherent sense of both the traditional uses and the preclinical biology.
Iridoid glycosides
This is probably the most pharmacologically significant compound class in the plant, and the one least likely to appear in popular foraging descriptions.
Iridoids are a class of cyclopentanoid monoterpenes — bicyclic ring structures — found widely across the Lamiaceae and other plant families.
A 2007 LC-ESI-MS study of Lamium species confirmed the presence of nine iridoids in L. purpureum: lamalbid, sesamoside, lamiol, 5-deoxylamiol, shanzhiside methyl ester, caryoptoside, penstemoside, lamioside, and barlerin.
In practical terms: iridoids are likely part of the reason this plant has been used historically for pain and inflammation.
Phenolic acids and flavonoids
The polyphenol profile is well-documented across several studies. Caffeic acid, chlorogenic acid, rutin, hyperoside, and quercetin have been identified in Romanian extract studies using HPTLC fingerprinting.
A 2022 multi-species study of European Lamium — which included L. purpureum — found these compounds present and active, with standardized extracts inhibiting TNF-α secretion in LPS-stimulated human immune cells.
The antioxidant activity itself has been measured across multiple studies using DPPH, ABTS, and FRAP assays.
Results are consistently solvent-dependent: methanol and other polar solvents extract the highest phenolic concentrations and the strongest measured activity.
Essential oil constituents — volatile chemistry
The volatile chemistry of purple dead-nettle is where the science gets genuinely complicated, because two major studies using different methods and different plant material have produced dramatically different results.
A 2005 Italian study analyzing volatiles from flowering aerial parts — including L. purpureum — found the dominant compound to be germacrene D, a sesquiterpene, at around 35% of the total volatile fraction.
The Lamiaceae is a family famous for chemotypic diversity — lavender, thyme, and oregano all show this — and there is no reason to assume Lamium purpureum is chemotypically uniform across its broad native and introduced range.
Phenylethanoid glycosides
This compound class — which includes verbascoside, one of the most studied secondary metabolites in the Lamiaceae — appears in cross-species Lamium literature.
Pollen chemistry
A Turkish bee-pollen study measuring protein content across taxa collected by honeybees found Lamium purpureum pollen among the highest-protein samples in their dataset.
Nutritional profile for human food use
Here the research base simply doesn't exist at the level of rigor it should.
What we have is a fodder composition study from Bulgaria — sun-dried hay from an organic farming system — reporting roughly 10% crude protein, 26% crude fibre, 2% crude fat, and 10% ash on a dry matter basis.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Addresses the critical safety considerations for using Purple Dead-Nettle, emphasizing the risk of heavy metal bioaccumulation in roadside plants. It provides guidance on proper identification to avoid taxonomic confusion and summarizes the known food and livestock safety profiles.
chunk_text:
Section 12 — Safety and Responsible Use

The safety profile of purple dead-nettle, considered honestly, is neither alarming nor unconditionally reassuring.
It is what you'd expect of a little-studied plant with a long history of edible and medicinal use: probably fine for most people in moderate food-use quantities, from appropriate sites, with basic identification competence — and genuinely uncertain beyond those parameters.
The contamination issue comes first, because it is the most practical risk
At least one analytical study — conducted in Kosovo, using ICP-MS methodology on plants including Lamium purpureum — documented measurable levels of arsenic, lead, and mercury in plant tissues from specimens growing near settlements, roads, and industrial areas, with some values exceeding referenced permissible levels.
The implications are straightforward. Purple dead-nettle grows enthusiastically in exactly the sites with the highest contamination risk: road shoulders, disturbed urban margins, old orchard edges, field borders adjacent to decades of agricultural chemical use.
The practical standard: harvest from sites with a known, clean soil history.
The look-alike situation for purple dead-nettle is not high-risk from a toxicity standpoint — the most likely confusion species, henbit (Lamium amplexicaule), is also non-toxic, and true stinging nettles (Urtica spp.) are not toxic, just unpleasant.
Human food safety
The edible-use record across multiple cultures and centuries is the strongest indirect evidence of safety at food quantities.
Specific cautions that apply to the plant in general but have not been evaluated specifically for L. purpureum: safety during pregnancy and lactation is unknown.
Anti-nutrient status is a genuine gap. Oxalate and nitrate content — both relevant to regular leafy green consumption — has not been measured for L. purpureum specifically in the primary literature.
Livestock
An extension weed management profile states clearly that purple dead-nettle has no reported toxic properties for ruminants, and a Bulgarian feeding study found rams were simply unenthusiastic about eating it — palatability was among the lowest of the weeds tested.
The contrast with henbit (Lamium amplexicaule) is worth noting: henbit has been associated with rare, reversible neurological effects in livestock grazing it heavily.
Young tops from clean, known-history soil, briefly cooked or eaten raw in small quantities, by healthy adults without specific contraindications: this is the space where the safety case is reasonable and the traditional use record provides meaningful support.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Explores the practical integration of Purple Dead-Nettle into regenerative farming systems. It emphasizes 'pollinator bridging,' the use of plant biomass for compost or mulching, and the role of volunteer cover in protecting soil during the cold months.
chunk_text:
Section 13 — Regenerative Farm Applications

The conversation about purple dead-nettle in regenerative systems is almost entirely a conversation about tradeoffs, and the tradeoffs are genuinely interesting.
Pollinator bridging — the strongest case
The most evidence-supported regenerative value for this plant is early-season pollinator forage, and the case is worth making carefully because it has direct economic and ecological implications for any operation with bees — managed or wild — in the landscape.
Purple dead-nettle flowers when almost nothing else does in the Pacific Northwest.
A stand of purple dead-nettle blooming in late February and March, in a market garden margin or orchard headland, is not a curiosity.
It is a functional piece of early-season bee nutrition infrastructure.
This approach — timed leaving followed by cut-and-remove — captures the pollinator value without compounding the seedbank.
Chop-and-drop and compost biomass
The spring flush of purple dead-nettle can produce meaningful above-ground biomass in a short window, and from clean sites this biomass has legitimate value as a compost or chop-and-drop input.
The plant is relatively nitrogen-rich for a weed — roughly 10% crude protein on a dry matter basis.
The seed-timing caveat here is critical and non-negotiable: once the plant is actively setting seed, cutting and leaving it in place means distributing seeds across your bed or pile.
Fermented plant extracts and botanical teas
There is genuine interest in using Lamium purpureum as a fermented plant extract (FPJ-style) or foliar preparation in Korean Natural Farming and related systems.
What we do know: the allelopathy question was addressed directly in one chemical ecology study using Alabama-collected plants. The finding was negative — the volatile oils did not explain allelopathic effects.
Soil and cover role
In the gap between autumn cultivation and spring planting, a low mat of purple dead-nettle does what cover cropping is designed to do: it covers bare soil, intercepts rainfall, and reduces surface erosion.
It does this for free, without seeding, and it's gone before you need the bed.
Vineyard and orchard applications
In vineyard and orchard contexts, the early-season pollinator argument is particularly compelling: fruit trees and early-flowering crops need pollinator activity precisely when purple dead-nettle is at peak bloom.
Where it fits best
Rough margins and headland strips managed for early pollinator value. Compost biomass from clean sites, cut before seed set.
Pulled — and bagged — wherever it appears in Garry Oak or native plant restoration contexts.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Provides practical instructions for harvesting and processing Purple Dead-Nettle for culinary and medicinal use. It covers fresh preparations, drying for tea, and tincturing, while clarifying the regulatory landscape for marketing products with therapeutic claims in Canada.
chunk_text:
Section 15 — Harvest, Preservation, and Product Possibilities

Harvest and preservation for purple dead-nettle is a topic where the practical traditional record is ahead of the formal scientific literature by a considerable margin.
Harvest — what, when, and where
The harvestable part is the young aerial tops: the growing tip with its crowded upper leaves, the tender stem below it, and — if you want them — the flowers, which are edible and mildly decorative.
Timing matters. The ideal harvest window is the period from first substantial leaf development through to the earliest stage of flowering — roughly late January through late February in southwestern BC.
Once flowering is well underway (mid to late March), the plant is still harvestable but increasingly fibrous at the stem.
Processing — fresh culinary use
Lightly wilted in butter or oil, or briefly blanched and squeezed dry, the plant softens into a mild, green, pleasantly unremarkable potherb.
Drying for tea
Drying for tea is a documented traditional preparation — Turkish ethnobotany used a decoction, and dried aerial parts are used in European herbal contexts.
General Lamiaceae drying principles apply: dry quickly at low temperatures (35–45°C), reduce to small bundles, and store in airtight containers.
Tincturing and extraction
If preparing an alcohol extract for medicinal or herbal use, polar solvents capture the phenolic and iridoid fractions most effectively.
Product possibilities
The most realistic product opportunities from purple dead-nettle, given current evidence and regulatory context, are at the food end of the spectrum rather than the medicinal end.
Health Canada's natural health product framework would require a product licence for any product making therapeutic claims.
The plant's story — early, overlooked, feeding the first bees, edible and mild — is itself a marketing asset in the right context.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Examines the economic value of Purple Dead-Nettle through cost-savings on pollinator forage and cover cropping. It also details the plant's legal status in British Columbia and the regulatory requirements for selling products in Canada.
chunk_text:
Section 16 — Economics and Livelihood

The economic conversation about purple dead-nettle is best entered from the side rather than head-on, because the direct revenue potential is modest and the indirect value — properly understood — is more interesting and more durable.
The most immediate economic value is in what you don't have to buy.
Early-season pollinator forage in a managed bee operation or a fruit-growing context has real dollar value.
A volunteer stand of purple dead-nettle in your orchard margins or farm headlands, providing pollen from late February onward, is doing work that would otherwise cost you something.
There is also the cover-function value. A volunteer cover of purple dead-nettle, managed with basic timing discipline, performs the soil-cover function without seeding costs or establishment effort.
Where it could make money
The most accessible opportunity is fresh spring greens, sold at a farmers market or through a restaurant account, framed as part of a broader wild or semi-wild spring mix.
The regenerative economics framework asks a question that conventional analysis doesn't: where does this plant create value that doesn't show up in any ledger? The answer is primarily in system resilience.
Section 17 — Legal and Ethical Note

As of March 2026, Lamium purpureum does not appear in Schedule A of BC's Weed Control Regulation — the provincial list of designated noxious weeds that triggers legal obligations for landowners to control.
The Garry Oak and associated ecosystems management materials treat it as an invasive alien species in those specific ecosystem contexts.
This is not a nuanced question: removing plants from BC Parks is generally illegal.
Selling a product with therapeutic claims immediately triggers Health Canada's Natural Health Products Regulations (SOR/2003-196), which requires a product licence.
The ethical question this plant raises most directly in the Pacific Northwest is the one about invasive species and our obligations to the ecosystems we steward.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Identifies high-leverage research gaps for Purple Dead-Nettle in the Pacific Northwest, such as regional chemotype mapping and pollinator visitation rates. It proposes practical citizen-science experiments for growers to contribute to the regional knowledge base.
chunk_text:
Section 18 — Research Frontiers and Citizen Science

The research frontier for this plant, in this bioregion, is essentially the entire field.
The most practically valuable research that could be done here falls into three clusters.
Chemistry and safety for BC populations. A targeted analytical study measuring iridoid glycosides and phenolic acids across BC-collected plant material would transform what we know about this plant's medicinal potential.
Pollinator ecology in BC context. A paired plot study in BC — measuring pollinator visitation rates and colony-level nutrition proxies — would provide the regional evidence base that management decisions currently lack.
Management outcomes in restoration contexts. Small replicated trials comparing pulling versus cutting versus mulching would provide the evidence base for more effective restoration management.
The allelopathy question is not closed by the negative volatile-oil study. Root exudates and competition dynamics remain untested for this species.
The essential oil chemotype question is wide open for Pacific Northwest populations.
Practical farm experiments worth running
Leave-and-cut trial: designate comparable patches in your headlands — some removed in January, some left until peak flowering then cut before seed set. Document pollinator activity.
Compost contribution: weigh and record the biomass harvested from a measured area at different harvest stages.
Ferment trial: prepare a standard FPJ-style preparation from early-spring tops and document visual outcomes against untreated controls.
The broader invitation is simply this: this plant is common, available, and understudied in your landscape.

source_document: Purple Dead-Nettle (Lamium purpureum) – Living Plant Wisdom Profile 2.0
summary: Synthesizes the broader 'wisdom' of Purple Dead-Nettle, interpreting its presence as a diagnostic for soil health and water retention. It reflects on the plant's strategy of temporal resilience and its value as an ecological anchor in a changing climate.
chunk_text:
Section 20 — Wisdom Synthesis

What this plant teaches about soil
Disturbed soil speaks. Purple dead-nettle is one of its clearest voices in the cool season — a reliable indicator that the ground has been opened, that nutrients are available at the surface, that the competitive vegetation community has been set back.
The deeper soil lesson is about cover. The fact that purple dead-nettle volunteers to cover bare ground in the cold months, without being asked, says something about how we've been trained to think about weeds.
What this plant teaches about water
The association with mesic conditions — adequate soil moisture, not waterlogged, not droughted — is precise.
Its presence in abundance suggests that the water-holding capacity of the soil is adequate, that rainfall is being retained rather than running off.
What this plant teaches about resilience
Temporal resilience — working the gap, blooming when nothing else does — is a different kind of resilience than persistence or hardiness.
Resilience doesn't always look like toughness. It sometimes looks like knowing exactly when to show up and exactly when to let go.
What this plant teaches about relationship
It reads the signal of soil disturbance more sensitively than most plants we deliberately introduce.
The more interesting response — not naïve acceptance, but attentive discernment — is to ask what this plant is doing in the specific place it appears.
What this plant may offer in a damaged future
As climate disruption continues to decouple phenological relationships, plants with strong internal clocks and cold-season commitments become more valuable.
There is a kind of plant knowledge that accumulates slowly, the way topsoil does.
The name says "dead." The plant says otherwise. Every February, it makes the argument that the gap between dormancy and awakening is not empty — that something is always working in the cold margins.
